name: julia
documentation:
PyJulia
=======

[![Stable documentation](https://img.shields.io/badge/docs-stable-blue.svg)](https://pyjulia.readthedocs.io/en/stable/)
[![Latest documentation](https://img.shields.io/badge/docs-latest-blue.svg)](https://pyjulia.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)
[![Main workflow](https://github.com/JuliaPy/pyjulia/workflows/Main%20workflow/badge.svg)](https://github.com/JuliaPy/pyjulia/actions?query=workflow%3A%22Main+workflow%22)
[![DOI](https://zenodo.org/badge/14576985.svg)](https://zenodo.org/badge/latestdoi/14576985)

Experimenting with developing a better interface to [Julia language](https://julialang.org/) that works with [Python](https://www.python.org/) 3 and Julia v1.4+.

Quick usage
-----------

```console
$ python3 -m pip install julia    # install PyJulia
...                               # you may need `--user` after `install`

$ python3
>>> import julia
>>> julia.install()               # install PyCall.jl etc.
>>> from julia import Base        # short demo
>>> Base.sind(90)
1.0
```

See more in the [documentation](https://pyjulia.readthedocs.io).

modules.name: julia.runtests
documentation: Run tests for PyJulia.

variables.name: julia.runtests.required_pytest
documentation:
Required pytest version.

This is a very loose lower bound because we abort `runtests` CLI if
this does not match.

functions.name: julia.juliainfo.samefile
documentation: For Python < 3.2 in Windows:

functions.name: julia.juliainfo.is_compatible_exe
documentation:
Determine if `libpython` is compatible with this Python.

Current Python executable is considered compatible if it is dynamically
linked to libpython and both of them are using identical libpython.  If
this function returns `True`, PyJulia use the same precompilation cache
of PyCall.jl used by Julia itself.

classes.name: julia.juliainfo.JuliaInfo

methods.name: julia.juliainfo.JuliaInfo.load
documentation: Get basic information from `julia`.

methods.name: julia.juliainfo.JuliaInfo.is_compatible_python
documentation: Check if python used by PyCall.jl is compatible with `sys.executable`.

Information required for initializing Julia runtime.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from julia.api import JuliaInfo
>>> info = JuliaInfo.load()
>>> info = JuliaInfo.load(julia="julia")  # equivalent
>>> info = JuliaInfo.load(julia="PATH/TO/julia")       # doctest: +SKIP
>>> info.julia
'julia'
>>> info.sysimage                                      # doctest: +SKIP
'/home/user/julia/lib/julia/sys.so'
>>> info.python                                        # doctest: +SKIP
'/usr/bin/python3'
>>> info.is_compatible_python()                        # doctest: +SKIP
True
```

# Attributes

julia

: Path to a Julia executable from which information was retrieved.

bindir

: `Sys.BINDIR` of `julia`.

libjulia_path

: Path to libjulia.

sysimage

: Path to system image.

python

: Python executable with which PyCall.jl is configured.

libpython_path

: libpython path used by PyCall.jl.

modules.name: julia.ipy.monkeypatch_completer
documentation:
Monkey-patch `IPCompleter` to make code completion work in `%%julia`.

This is done by monkey-patching because it looks like there is no
immediate plan for an API to do this:
<https://github.com/ipython/ipython/pull/10722>

functions.name: julia.tools.install
documentation:
install(*, julia="julia", color="auto")
Install Julia packages required by PyJulia in `julia`.

This function installs and/or re-builds PyCall if necessary.  It
also makes sure to build PyCall in a way compatible with this
Python executable (if possible).

Keyword Arguments
-----------------
julia : str
    Julia executable (default: "julia")
color : "auto", False or True
    Use colorful output if `True`.  "auto" (default) to detect it
    automatically.

classes.name: julia.libjulia.LibJulia

methods.name: julia.libjulia.LibJulia.load
documentation:
Create `LibJulia` based on information retrieved with `JuliaInfo.load`.

This classmethod runs `JuliaInfo.load` to retrieve information about
`julia` runtime.  This information is used to intialize `LibJulia`.

methods.name: julia.libjulia.LibJulia.init_julia
documentation:
Initialize `libjulia`. Calling this method twice is a no-op.

It calls `jl_init_with_image` (or `jl_init_with_image__threading`)
but makes sure that it is called only once for each process.

# Parameters

options

: This is passed as command line options to the Julia runtime.

  :::{warning}
  Any invalid command line option terminates the entire
  Python process.
  :::

Low-level interface to `libjulia` C-API.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from julia.api import LibJulia, JuliaInfo
```

An easy way to create a `LibJulia` object is `LibJulia.load`:

```pycon
>>> api = LibJulia.load()                              # doctest: +SKIP
```

Or, equivalently,

```pycon
>>> api = LibJulia.load(julia="julia")                 # doctest: +SKIP
>>> api = LibJulia.from_juliainfo(JuliaInfo.load())    # doctest: +SKIP
```

You can pass a path to the Julia executable using `julia` keyword
argument:

```pycon
>>> api = LibJulia.load(julia="PATH/TO/CUSTOM/julia")  # doctest: +SKIP
```

% Do not run doctest with non-default libjulia.so.
% >>> _ = getfixture("julia")
% >>> api = get_libjulia()

Path to the system image can be configured before initializing Julia:

```pycon
>>> api.sysimage                                       # doctest: +SKIP
'/home/user/julia/lib/julia/sys.so'
>>> api.sysimage = "PATH/TO/CUSTOM/sys.so"             # doctest: +SKIP
```

Finally, the Julia runtime can be initialized using `LibJulia.init_julia`.
Note that only the first call to this function in the current Python
process takes effect.

```pycon
>>> api.init_julia()
```

Any command-line options supported by Julia can be passed to
`init_julia`:

```pycon
>>> api.init_julia(["--compiled-modules=no", "--optimize=3"])
```

Once `init_julia` is called, any subsequent use of `Julia` API
(thus also `from julia import <JuliaModule>` etc.) uses this
initialized Julia runtime.

`LibJulia` can be used to access Julia's C-API:

```pycon
>>> ret = api.jl_eval_string(b"Int64(1 + 2)")
>>> int(api.jl_unbox_int64(ret))
3
```

However, a proper use of the C-API is more involved and presumably
very challenging without C macros. See also:
<https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/embedding/>.

# Attributes

libjulia_path

: Path to libjulia.

bindir

: `Sys.BINDIR` of `julia`. This is passed to
  `jl_init_with_image` unless overridden by argument `option`
  to `init_julia`.

sysimage

: Path to system image. This is passed to `jl_init_with_image`
  unless overridden by argument `option` to `init_julia`.

  If `sysimage` is a relative path, it is interpreted relative
  to the current directory (rather than relative to the Julia
  `bindir` as in the `jl_init_with_image` C API).

modules.name: julia.pseudo_python_cli
documentation:
Pseudo Python command line interface.

It tries to mimic a subset of Python CLI:
https://docs.python.org/3/using/cmdline.html

classes.name: julia.pseudo_python_cli.PyArgumentParser

methods.name: julia.pseudo_python_cli.PyArgumentParser._add_argument_impl
documentation: fmt: off

methods.name: julia.pseudo_python_cli.PyArgumentParser._find_matches
documentation:
Return a list of `.Result`.

If value presents in `arg` (i.e., `--long-option=value`), it
becomes the element of `.Result.values` (a list). Otherwise,
this list has to be filled by the caller (`_parse_until_terminal`).

methods.name: julia.pseudo_python_cli.PyArgumentParser.print_usage
documentation: fmt: on

`ArgumentParser`-like parser with "terminal option" support.

Major differences:

* Formatted help has to be provided to `description`.
* Many options for `.add_argument` are not supported.
* Especially, there is no positional argument support: all positional
  arguments go into `ns.args`.
* `.add_argument` can take boolean option `terminal` (default: `False`)
  to stop parsing after consuming the given option.

functions.name: julia.tests.test_python_jl.test_help_option
documentation: fmt: off

modules.name: julia.python_jl
documentation:
Python interpreter inside a Julia process.

This command line interface mimics a basic subset of Python program so that
Python program involving calls to Julia functions can be run in a *Julia*
process. This avoids the known problem with pre-compilation cache in
Debian-based distribution such as Ubuntu and Python executable installed by
Conda in Linux.

Although this script has -i option and it can do a basic REPL, contrl-c may
crash the whole process. Consider using IPython >= 7 which can be launched
by:

```
python-jl -m IPython
```

:::{NOTE}
For this command to work, Python environment with which PyCall.jl is
configured has to have PyJulia installed.
:::

functions.name: julia.python_jl.remove_julia_options
documentation:
Remove options used in this Python process.

```pycon
>>> list(remove_julia_options(["a", "b", "c"]))
['a', 'b', 'c']
>>> list(remove_julia_options(["a", "--julia", "julia", "b", "c"]))
['a', 'b', 'c']
>>> list(remove_julia_options(["a", "b", "c", "--julia=julia"]))
['a', 'b', 'c']
```

functions.name: julia.python_jl.parse_pyjl_args
documentation:
Return a pair of parsed result and "unused" arguments.

# Returns

ns

: Parsed result. Only `ns.julia` is relevant here.

unused_args

: Arguments to be parsed (again) by `.pseudo_python_cli.main`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> ns, unused_args = parse_pyjl_args([])
>>> ns.julia
'julia'
>>> unused_args
[]
>>> ns, unused_args = parse_pyjl_args(
...     ["--julia", "julia-dev", "-i", "-c", "import julia"])
>>> ns.julia
'julia-dev'
>>> unused_args
['-i', '-c', 'import julia']
```

modules.name: julia.with_rebuilt
documentation:
(Maybe) Re-build PyCall.jl to test `exe_differs=False` path.

`Pkg.build("PyCall")` is run on Julia side when the environment
variable `PYJULIA_TEST_REBUILD` is set to `yes`.

functions.name: julia.with_rebuilt.maybe_rebuild
documentation: fmt: off

functions.name: julia.with_rebuilt.ignoring
documentation:
Context manager for ignoring signal `sig`.

For example,:

```
with ignoring(signal.SIGINT):
    do_something()
```

would ignore user's ctrl-c during `do_something()`. This is
useful when launching interactive program (in which ctrl-c is a
valid keybinding) from Python.

functions.name: julia.tests.utils.retry_failing_if_windows
documentation:
Retry upon test failure if in Windows.

This is an ugly workaround for occasional STATUS_ACCESS_VIOLATION failures
in Windows: https://github.com/JuliaPy/pyjulia/issues/462

functions.name: julia.tests.test_compatible_exe.runcode
documentation: Run `code` in `python`.

functions.name: julia.tests.test_compatible_exe.is_dynamically_linked
documentation:
Check if Python `executable` is (likely to be) dynamically linked.

It returns three possible values:

* `True`: Likely that it's dynamically linked.
* `False`: Likely that it's statically linked.
* `None`: Unsupported platform.

It's only "likely" since the check is by simple occurrence of a
some substrings like "libpython".  For example, if there is
another library existing on the path containing "libpython", this
function may return false-positive.

functions.name: julia.tests.test_compatible_exe.test_statically_linked
documentation:
Simulate the case PyCall is configured with statically linked Python.

In this case, `find_libpython()` would return the path identical
to the one in PyCall's deps.jl.  `is_compatible_exe` should reject
it.

even prior to IPython 7.3, *cell* magic never did the string interpolation, so
below, any time we need to test $x interpolation, do it as cell magic so it

modules.name: julia.sysimage
documentation:
Build system image.

Example:

```
python3 -m julia.sysimage sys.so
```

Generated system image can be passed to `sysimage` option of
`julia.api.Julia`.

:::{note}
This script is not tested on Windows.
:::

modules.name: julia.julia_py
documentation:
Launch Julia through PyJulia.

Currently, `julia-py` is primary used internally for supporting
`julia.sysimage` command line interface. Using `julia-py` like normal
Julia program requires `--sysimage` to be set to the system image
created by `julia.sysimage`.

Example:

```
$ python3 -m julia.sysimage sys.so
$ julia-py --sysimage sys.so
```

functions.name: julia.julia_py.is_pyjulia_in_julia_debug
documentation:
Parse `JULIA_DEBUG` and return the debug flag.

```pycon
>>> is_pyjulia_in_julia_debug("")
False
>>> is_pyjulia_in_julia_debug("Main,loading")
False
>>> is_pyjulia_in_julia_debug("pyjulia")
True
>>> is_pyjulia_in_julia_debug("all")
True
>>> is_pyjulia_in_julia_debug("all,!pyjulia")
False
```

Ref:
<https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/32432>

Find the linked libpython using dladdr (in *nix).

Returns

path : str or None
    A path to linked libpython.  Return `None` if statically linked.

functions.name: julia.find_libpython._linked_libpython_unix
documentation: fmt: off

functions.name: julia.find_libpython._linked_libpython_windows
documentation: Based on: https://stackoverflow.com/a/16659821

functions.name: julia.find_libpython.library_name
documentation:
Convert a file basename `name` to a library name (no "lib" and ".so" etc.)

```pycon
>>> library_name("libpython3.7m.so")                   # doctest: +SKIP
'python3.7m'
>>> library_name("libpython3.7m.so", suffix=".so", is_windows=False)
'python3.7m'
>>> library_name("libpython3.7m.dylib", suffix=".dylib", is_windows=False)
'python3.7m'
>>> library_name("python37.dll", suffix=".dll", is_windows=True)
'python37'
```

functions.name: julia.find_libpython.uniquifying
documentation:
Yield items while excluding the duplicates and preserving the order.

```pycon
>>> list(uniquifying([1, 2, 1, 2, 3]))
[1, 2, 3]
```

functions.name: julia.find_libpython.uniquified
documentation: Wrap iterator returned from `func` by `uniquifying`.

functions.name: julia.find_libpython.candidate_names
documentation:
Iterate over candidate file names of libpython.

Yields
------
name : str
    Candidate name libpython.

functions.name: julia.find_libpython.candidate_paths
documentation:
Iterate over candidate paths of libpython.

Yields
------
path : str or None
    Candidate path to libpython.  The path may not be a fullpath
    and may not exist.

functions.name: julia.find_libpython.normalize_path
documentation:
Normalize shared library `path` to a real path.

If `path` is not a full path, `None` is returned.  If `path` does
not exists, append `SHLIB_SUFFIX` and check if it exists.
Finally, the path is canonicalized by following the symlinks.

Parameters
----------
path : str or None
    A candidate path to a shared library.

Returns
-------
path : str or None
    Normalized existing path or `None`.
Possibly useful links:
* https://packages.ubuntu.com/bionic/amd64/libpython3.6/filelist
* https://github.com/Valloric/ycmd/issues/518
* https://github.com/Valloric/ycmd/pull/519

functions.name: julia.find_libpython._remove_suffix_apple
documentation:
Strip off .so or .dylib.

```pycon
>>> _remove_suffix_apple("libpython.so")
'libpython'
>>> _remove_suffix_apple("libpython.dylib")
'libpython'
>>> _remove_suffix_apple("libpython3.7")
'libpython3.7'
```

functions.name: julia.find_libpython.finding_libpython
documentation:
Iterate over existing libpython paths.

The first item is likely to be the best one.

Yields
------
path : str
    Existing path to a libpython.

functions.name: julia.find_libpython.find_libpython
documentation:
Return a path (`str`) to libpython or `None` if not found.

Parameters
----------
path : str or None
    Existing path to the (supposedly) correct libpython.

functions.name: julia.options.parse_jl_options
documentation:
Parse --home and --sysimage options.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> ns = parse_jl_options(["--home", "PATH/TO/HOME"])
>>> ns
Namespace(home='PATH/TO/HOME', sysimage=None)
>>> ns.home
'PATH/TO/HOME'
>>> parse_jl_options([])
Namespace(home=None, sysimage=None)
>>> parse_jl_options(["-HHOME", "--sysimage=PATH/TO/sys.so"])
Namespace(home='HOME', sysimage='PATH/TO/sys.so')
```

Attributes

modules.name: julia.core
documentation: Bridge Python and Julia by initializing the Julia runtime inside Python.

functions.name: julia.core.get_loghandler
documentation: Get `logging.StreamHandler` private to PyJulia.

functions.name: julia.core.ismacro
documentation:
Is the name a macro?

```pycon
>>> ismacro('@time')
True
>>> ismacro('sum')
False
```

functions.name: julia.core.is_accessible_name
documentation:
Check if a Julia variable `name` is (easily) accessible from Python.

Return `True` if `name` can be safely converted to a Python
identifier using `py_name` function. For example,

```pycon
>>> is_accessible_name('A_mul_B!')
True
```

Since it can be accessed as `A_mul_B_b` in Python.

classes.name: julia.core.Julia

methods.name: julia.core.Julia.__init__
documentation:
Create a Python object that represents a live Julia runtime.

Note: Use `LibJulia` to fully control the initialization of
the Julia runtime.

Parameters
==========

init_julia : bool
    If True, try to initialize the Julia runtime. If this code is
    being called from inside an already running Julia, the flag should
    be passed as False so the interpreter isn't re-initialized.

    Note that it is safe to call this class constructor twice in the
    same process with `init_julia` set to True, as a global reference
    is kept to avoid re-initializing it. The purpose of the flag is
    only to manage situations when Julia was initialized from outside
    this code.

runtime : str
    Custom Julia binary, e.g. "/usr/local/bin/julia" or "julia-1.0.0".

debug : bool
    If True, print some debugging information to STDERR
fmt: off

methods.name: julia.core.Julia._call
documentation:
Low-level call to execute a snippet of Julia source.

This only raises an exception if Julia itself throws an error, but it
does NO type conversion into usable Python objects nor any memory
management. It should never be used for returning the result of Julia
expressions, only to execute statements.

methods.name: julia.core.Julia._unbox_as
documentation: `_unbox_as` was added for communicating with Julia runtime before
initializing PyCal:
* Fail with a helpful message if separate cache is not supported
https://github.com/JuliaPy/pyjulia/pull/186
However, this is not used anymore at the moment. Maybe clean this up?

methods.name: julia.core.Julia.help
documentation: Return help string for function by name.

methods.name: julia.core.Julia.eval
documentation: Execute code in Julia, then pull some results back to Python.

methods.name: julia.core.Julia.using
documentation: Load module in Julia by calling the `using module` command
fmt: on

Implements a bridge to the Julia runtime.
This uses the Julia PyCall module to perform type conversions and allow
full access to the entire Julia runtime.
